lesson_title: Introduction to std::vector and list constructors

cards.text: To use std::vector, include the {{c1::<vector>}} header.
cards.source: 16.2 - Introduction to std::vector and list constructors

cards.text:
To declare an empty std::vector of integers: {{c1::std::vector<int> v;}}
To declare with specific values: {{c2::std::vector<int> v{ 1, 2, 3 };}}
cards.source: 16.2 - Introduction to std::vector and list constructors

cards.front: What is the difference between std::vector<int> v(5) and std::vector<int> v{5}?
cards.back:
std::vector<int> v(5) creates a vector with 5 value-initialized elements (all zeros).
std::vector<int> v{5} creates a vector with one element: the value 5.
cards.source: 16.2 - Introduction to std::vector and list constructors

cards.text: To create a vector with 10 elements, each initialized to 7: {{c1::std::vector<int> v(10, 7);}}
cards.source: 16.2 - Introduction to std::vector and list constructors

cards.question: What's the problem with this code?
cards.answer: No problem - this creates a vector with 5 elements, each initialized to 10. Outputs: 5
cards.explanation: The parenthesis initialization syntax (count, value) is valid. This creates 5 elements with value 10.
cards.source: 16.2 - Introduction to std::vector and list constructors

cards.front: What is list initialization (or uniform initialization)?
cards.back: A C++11 feature using curly braces {} to initialize objects. For containers, it allows initializing with a list of values: std::vector<int> v{1, 2, 3, 4};
cards.source: 16.2 - Introduction to std::vector and list constructors

cards.text: To access an element in a vector by index, use the {{c1::subscript operator []}} or the {{c2::at()}} member function.
cards.source: 16.2 - Introduction to std::vector and list constructors

cards.front: What is the difference between vector[index] and vector.at(index)?
cards.back:
vector[index] does not perform bounds checking (undefined behavior if out of range).
vector.at(index) performs bounds checking and throws std::out_of_range exception if index is invalid.
cards.source: 16.2 - Introduction to std::vector and list constructors

cards.text: To get the length of a vector, use the {{c1::size()}} or {{c1::length()}} member function.
cards.source: 16.2 - Introduction to std::vector and list constructors

cards.concept: Why use brace initialization for std::vector when possible
cards.explanation:
Brace initialization (list initialization) is preferred because:
  • More intuitive syntax for initializing with values
  • Consistent with modern C++ style
  • Avoids confusion with constructor overloads
  • Cannot narrow (safer)
cards.source: 16.2 - Introduction to std::vector and list constructors

cards.text: std::vector is a {{c1::class template}}, so you must specify the {{c2::element type}} as a template argument: std::vector<{{c2::int}}>.
cards.source: 16.2 - Introduction to std::vector and list constructors

cards.question: What does this code output?
cards.explanation:
v1 uses brace initialization: creates 1 element with value 5.
v2 uses parenthesis: creates 5 value-initialized elements.
cards.source: 16.2 - Introduction to std::vector and list constructors
